name: Domino Reduction
description: Domino Reduction is a two-phase method that first reduces the cube to a 'domino state' where all pieces are oriented and E-slice edges are separated, then solves the cube using only half-turn moves on the restricted axes. It is named because the reduced state resembles the constraints of a domino puzzle. This method is widely used in Fewest Moves Challenge (FMC) as the first phase of many competitive solutions, and its concepts underpin Thistlethwaite's and Kociemba's computer algorithms.

steps.name: Edge Orientation
steps.description: Orient all 12 edges so that none require an F or B quarter turn to be solved. Bad edges are identified and flipped using F/B moves, after which F and B are restricted to double turns only.
tips: Use the standard F/B axis rule to identify bad edges
This is identical to EO in ZZ and Petrus methods
Fewer bad edges means fewer moves needed
In FMC, try multiple EO solutions to find short ones

steps.name: Corner Orientation + E-slice Separation
steps.description: Orient all 8 corners (so all U/D stickers face U or D) and separate the 4 E-slice edges into the E-slice. The E-slice edges do not need to be permuted correctly yet. After this step, the cube is in the 'domino' state.
tips: Use R and L moves with U/D adjustments for CO
E-slice edges can often be placed during CO
The domino state is the key milestone of this method
In FMC, this phase is often combined with EO using NISS

steps.name: Domino Solve (Half-Turn Phase)
steps.description: Solve the cube completely using only R2, L2, F2, B2, U, and D moves (half turns on R/L/F/B, any turns on U/D). All pieces are in their correct orbits, so the remaining solve is constrained to this reduced move group.
tips: Think of this as solving a simpler puzzle within the cube
Corner permutation and edge permutation can be solved separately
Many FMC solvers use blockbuilding in the domino state
Half-turn moves are easy to execute quickly

pros: Clean theoretical framework based on group theory
Domino state is a powerful intermediate goal
Concepts transfer to many other methods
cons: CO + E-slice separation is challenging to plan
Not efficient for speedsolving due to planning overhead
Requires understanding of piece orientation theory
Half-turn phase still requires significant thought
Better suited for FMC than timed solving
